question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Short summary - Your symptom (you can hear others, others can’t hear you; Teams shows the USB headset selected but the mic/input bar never moves and Test Call records nothing) most commonly indicates a local hardware or OS-level capture problem — often fixed by first-line (helpdesk) actions. In one documented case the headset was visible to Teams and Windows but the mic cable inside the headset was not fully inserted; reseating that cable immediately restored the Windows input meter and the Teams Test Call succeeded
context: SOURCE 37: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Call participants cannot hear the user Ticket number: TI-1616128 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 07 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Telephone Type: Incident Created on: 14.10.25 09:18 Closure Closure date: 14.10.25 09:52 Closure text: Microphone not properly connected. Device configured correctly. Function restored. Activities 14.10.25 09:10 – Telephone report Mr. Contact Person 07 reports that an employee can hear everything during Teams calls but cannot be heard by the other participant. Both internal and external calls are affected. Chat works. Camera works. 14.10.25 09:18 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 33. 14.10.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good morning Could you please briefly confirm: – Is a headset or the integrated microphone being used? – Does the issue also occur during a Teams test call? Thank you. 14.10.25 09:32 – Customer response A USB headset is being used. No sound is recorded during the test call either. 14.10.25 09:40 – Remote session Connected with the user. In Teams, “USB Audio Device” is selected as the microphone. However, the input level indicator remains empty. In the Windows Sound menu, no input signal is displayed either. After a brief check, it was determined that although the USB headset was plugged in, the microphone cable on the headset itself was not fully inserted. After correctly plugging it in, the input level indicator immediately showed activity. Test call performed → Microphone functioning. 14.10.25 09:50 – User feedback Call tested. Working properly again. 14.10.25 09:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 07 The microphone was not properly connected, which prevented any input signal from being detected. After correctly reconnecting the device, voice transmission is functioning normally again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 44: # USB Headset Microphone Failure — USB Audio Device & Microphone Cable on Windows Workstation This community centers on a single-user Windows workstation using a USB headset whose microphone produced no input until the headset microphone cable was reseated. The USB headset (containing the microphone and cable) was selected in Teams as the "USB Audio Device" but showed no input levels in both Teams and the Windows Sound menu, which was used to validate the failure at the OS/device level; reseating the microphone cable restored audio. Key entities: USB HEADSET, MICROPHONE, MICROPHONE CABLE, USB AUDIO DEVICE (Teams selection), and WINDOWS SOUND MENU (OS-level verification). [Data: Entities (667,668,669,670,671), Relationships (1171,1174,1176,1177)] ## Root cause: physical microphone cable not fully inserted The immediate cause of the no-audio condition was a partially disconnected microphone cable in the headset assembly; reseating the cable restored microphone functionality. This is directly supported by the relationship that links the MICROPHONE to the MICROPHONE CABLE and documents that the cable was not fully inserted and that reseating restored operation. [Data: Entities (668,669,667), Relationships (1174,1171)] Because the microphone element is part of the USB HEADSET and the cable is the physical connection carrying the mic signal, a poor connection at the cable interface explains why neither the OS nor Teams observed input until the hardware was corrected. The dataset records the headset contains the microphone and that the microphone cable was found to be not fully connected, establishing a clear hardware root cause. [Data: Entities (667,668,669), Relationships (1170,1174,1171)] ## Symptom confirmation at OS level — issue not limited to Teams The USB audio device selected in Teams showed no input levels in the Windows Sound menu, which was used to confirm the absence and subsequent restoration of the microphone signal at the operating-system/device level. This indicates the fault manifested below the application layer rather than being a Teams-only configuration problem. [Data: Entities (670,671), Relationships (1176,1177)] Operationally, using the Windows Sound menu as an independent verification step validated that the device itself was not producing audio input until the hardware fix; this diagnostic step is recorded and tied to the user's workstation. The Windows Sound menu therefore provided reliable evidence to rule out application-level causes. [Data: Entities (671,670), Relationships (1177,1176)] ## Application impact: Teams input selection matched the faulty device Teams had the USB headset selected as the input device ("USB Audio Device") but showed no input until the headset cable was reseated; the data records that the same device selected in Teams produced no input in the Windows Sound menu, confirming consistent behaviour across layers. This supports the conclusion that Teams was correctly configured but received no signal due to a hardware issue. [Data: Entities (670,667,671), Relationships (1176)] For troubleshooting, this pattern (application selection correct + no OS-level input) is an important triage signal: it directs responders to verify physical connections and device-level diagnostics before performing application reconfiguration or account-level changes. [Data: Entities (670,671), Relationships (1176,1177)] ## Scope and operational risk: single-user incident with localized service impact The dataset ties the Windows Sound menu observation to the user's Windows workstation, indicating this was a workstation-level, single-user incident rather than a wider service or tenant outage. The relationships capture that the Windows Sound menu was used on the user's workstation to observe the absence and restoration of the microphone signal. [Data: Relationships (1177), Entities (667)] Although the incident impacted real-time communications (Teams calls) for that user, the evidence shows no systemic failure across other devices or services; therefore operational risk is localized but user-impacting for collaboration and meeting availability. Incident remediation was quick once hardware was addressed, reducing broader business continuity concerns. [Data: Entities (667,670), Relationships (1176,1177)] ## Recommended immediate runbook: hardware-first triage with OS verification Triage should follow a hardware-first sequence: (1) verify headset and mic cable physical seating; (2) confirm device presence and input level in Windows Sound menu; (3) confirm application selection (e.g., Teams "USB Audio Device"); (4) reseat or replace cable/headset if no input; (5) escalate to device replacement if intermittent. The dataset documents that reseating the microphone cable restored functionality and that the Windows Sound menu was used to confirm the absence/presence of input, supporting these exact steps. [Data: Entities (669,671,667), Relationships (1171,1174,1177)] Documenting and distributing this runbook to IT support will shorten mean time to repair for similar incidents and avoids unnecessary changes in application configuration or account-level actions when the root cause is physical hardware. The evidence shows a straightforward hardware fix resolved the symptom, so the runbook should prioritize physical checks first. [Data: Entities (667,668,669), Relationships (1171,1174)] ## Preventive actions and governance to reduce recurrence Given the root cause, low-effort preventive controls will reduce recurrence: include headset seating checks in user self-help guides, require spare headsets for quick swap, and add a brief hardware-connection checklist to onboarding and support scripts. The rationale for these measures is grounded in the recorded failure mode (microphone cable not fully inserted) and the rapid return to service after reseating. [Data: Entities (669,667), Relationships (1171,1174)] Operationally, procurement standards could specify headsets with integrated, secure mic connectors or fewer detachable interfaces if the organization sees repeated similar faults across users. While the dataset describes a single event, the clear hardware failure pattern justifies lightweight governance to reduce repetitive support effort and user downtime. [Data: Entities (667,669)] ## Monitoring and detection: leverage OS diagnostics for quicker isolation The Windows Sound menu provided reliable confirmation that the issue existed at the device/OS layer; incorporating an explicit diagnostic step to check OS-level input indicators into support scripts and monitoring tools will speed isolation between hardware and application causes. The recorded use of the Windows Sound menu to observe absence and restoration supports formalizing this as part of the triage flow. [Data: Entities (671,670), Relationships (1177,1176)] Where feasible, endpoint management tooling could be extended to report device input status or recent device disconnects for headsets, enabling proactive detection of flaky connections before users escalate. The current evidence demonstrates that OS-level visibility is sufficient to distinguish hardware faults from application misconfiguration in these cases. [Data: Entities (671,670), Relationships (1176,1177)]

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . Immediate steps to run now (do these in order) 1. Check the Windows input meter first: Settings → System → Sound → Input (or Control Panel → Sound → Recording). Speak and watch whether any input bar moves. 2. If the Windows meter is flat: fully unplug the headset, reseat any detachable mic/boom connector on the headset (if present), and plug the headset directly into a PC USB port (bypass hubs/docks). Try a different physical USB port (rear/host port preferred). Reseating the mic cable fixed the cited case

answer: . 3. If still flat, test the same headset on another PC (and/or try a known-good headset on this PC) to isolate headset vs. PC. The results of these swaps determine next steps. 4. If the Windows meter moves but Teams still records nothing: verify Windows Microphone privacy (Settings → Privacy & security → Microphone) and ensure Teams is allowed to use the mic, then restart Teams and re-run a Test Call. OS privacy settings have caused similar device-access issues in our records
context: SOURCE 42: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Camera not detected Ticket number: TI-1615042 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 09 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 07.10.25 09:12 Closure Closure date: 07.10.25 11:06 Closure text: Camera available again in Teams. Root cause was missing operating system permission and blocked device setting. Steps performed according to Blueprint. Activities 07.10.25 09:05 – Email from Contact Person 09 Good morning For one user, the camera is no longer working in Teams. No camera is displayed in the Teams device settings. In other apps it also appears to be partially unavailable. Could you please assist? Kind regards 07.10.25 09:12 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 33. 07.10.25 09:30 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please confirm whether the user is using Windows or Mac and whether the camera is available in the browser (e.g., Teams Web)? Thank you. 07.10.25 09:48 – Customer response Windows 11. The camera also does not work in the browser. 07.10.25 10:25 – Internal note Connected to the user via remote session. The camera is visible in Device Manager, but Teams has no access. In Windows privacy settings, camera access for apps is disabled. Additionally, Teams is not enabled in the list of allowed apps. According to “Blueprint – Teams Client Devices & Permissions – Version 1.2”, the standard checks were performed (OS privacy settings, app permissions, Teams restart). 07.10.25 10:55 – Customer feedback User confirms that the camera is now displayed in Teams and the test call is successful. 07.10.25 11:06 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 09 The camera was blocked by operating system permissions. After adjusting the camera and app permissions according to the Blueprint, the camera is available again in Teams. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - Escalate to 2nd‑level (UC/network or endpoint engineering) if ANY of the following apply: the Windows input meter remains flat after trying multiple ports and another known-good headset; Device Manager shows driver errors you can’t resolve; multiple users at the same site have identical symptoms (site/network/media path issue); or the OS shows input but Teams still won’t transmit after permissions and client checks. Site‑wide or Direct‑Routing/SBC/firewall media issues required 2nd‑level/UC and network work in other incidents
context: SOURCE 23: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls without audio (only silence) Ticket number: TI-1602241 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 14 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 08.07.25 08:21 Closure Closure date: 18.07.25 16:05S Closure text: Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to Direct Routing architecture. Voice transmission stable again. Activities 08.07.25 08:10 Phone notification. Several employees report: • External calls can be established • The other party can hear them • They themselves hear nothing (complete silence) Internal Teams-to-Teams calls function. Chat and video also function. Affected: approx. 12 users at the Zurich location. 08.07.25 08:21 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 33 Status: In Progress Notes 08.07.25 09:05 Helpdesk asks: • Does it affect only external PSTN calls? • Only incoming or also outgoing? • Headset or speaker? • WLAN or LAN? 08.07.25 09:30 Customer responds: • Only external calls affected • Incoming and outgoing • Various headsets • LAN connection • Other locations not affected 08.07.25 10:15 Helpdesk tests with affected user: Test 1 – Internal Teams call → OK Test 2 – External mobile call → No audio Test 3 – External landline call → No audio Notable: Call status shows “Connected”. 08.07.25 11:40 Initial hypothesis: Client issue Measures: • Teams cache cleared • Teams reinstalled • Drivers checked Result: No change. 09.07.25 08:55 Customer adds: A new firewall rule was implemented at the location over the weekend (Security Hardening). Unclear whether there is a correlation. 09.07.25 10:10 Helpdesk checks: • Internet stable • No packet loss visible • Microsoft 365 status normal Suspicion: Network / VoIP layer Ticket forwarded to: BU Unified Communications 09.07.25 10:22 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 18 In-depth analysis 09.07.25 11:15 Analysis in Teams Admin Center: • Direct Routing active • SBC status: Online • PSTN calls are signaled • No errors in SIP signaling Notable: • RTP stream appears not to run bidirectionally 10.07.25 08:40 SBC log analysis: • SIP INVITE correct • 200 OK correct • Media negotiation (SDP) successful RTP stream from Microsoft IP visible RTP stream from internal client not consistently visible 10.07.25 10:50 Firewall logs reviewed. New rule implemented: Outbound traffic restricted Only defined port ranges allowed Documentation shows: Teams media ports UDP 3478–3481 and dynamic RTP ports 49152–53247 Firewall rule allows only 3478–3481. 11.07.25 09:15 Test: Temporary opening of wider UDP port range → Audio works immediately. Port restricted again → No audio. 11.07.25 13:20 Comparison with internal architecture documentation: Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Media Flow – Version 2.1” Section 3 describes: • Media Bypass behavior • RTP port range requirements • Location-specific firewall rules • NAT traversal 14.07.25 Comparison shows: Zurich location uses Media Bypass Other locations do not. With Media Bypass, RTP runs directly from client to SBC. Firewall rule was designed only for signaling. 16.07.25 Configuration adjustment carried out according to architecture specification. Details not documented in the ticket for security reasons. 18.07.25 15:30 Multiple external test calls successful. Audio stable in both directions. Monitoring 48h without anomalies. Ticket closure 18.07.25 16:05 Dear Contact Person 14 The root cause was a restriction of media port communication following a firewall adjustment at the Zurich location. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Voice transmission is stable again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 5: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – No audio in external calls Ticket number: TI-1602241 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 14 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 08.07.25 08:21 Closure Closure date: 18.07.25 16:05 Closure text: Configuration adjusted according to Direct Routing architecture. Voice transmission stable again. Activities 08.07.25 08:10 Call from Mr. Contact Person 14. He reports that several employees since this morning cannot hear audio during external phone calls in Microsoft Teams. The call is established, the external participant can hear the employees, but they themselves hear nothing. It is described as “completely silent”. Internal Teams calls function normally. Chats and video conferences are also not affected. 08.07.25 08:21 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 33. 08.07.25 09:05 – Email from Employee 33 to Contact Person 14 Good morning Mr. Contact Person 14 Thank you for your report. Could you please briefly confirm the following points: – Does it affect only external calls (mobile/fixed line)? – Are both incoming and outgoing calls affected? – Does the issue occur for all users or only specific ones? – Since when has the issue existed? Thank you for your feedback. Kind regards 08.07.25 09:34 – Response from Contact Person 14 Good morning Yes, it affects only external calls. Both incoming and outgoing. At the moment, about a dozen people are affected, all at the Zurich location. Since this morning, everything was normal yesterday. Kind regards 08.07.25 10:12 – Internal note Test call performed with user S. Meier. Internal Teams call works. External call to mobile phone: connection established, conversation partner confirms he can hear Mr. Meier, Mr. Meier hears nothing. Teams shows status “Connected”. No error message. 08.07.25 11:40 – Email to customer We have restarted the Teams client and cleared the cache as a test. Could you please check with two additional affected users whether the behavior is identical? 08.07.25 13:05 – Customer response We tested with three additional persons. Same behavior everywhere. Headsets differ (Jabra, Poly). LAN connection. Home office users do not seem to be affected. 09.07.25 08:50 – Customer contacts again Good morning We just remembered that a firewall adjustment (security hardening) was carried out over the weekend. Could this have an impact? Kind regards 09.07.25 09:30 – Internal note Firewall change timing plausible. Escalated to Unified Communications / Network team. 09.07.25 10:22 – Ticket taken over by System Engineer 18 09.07.25 11:15 – Email from System Engineer 18 to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing configuration. Signaling (SIP) appears to function correctly, as calls can be established. We are currently checking the media flow (audio stream). We will get back to you as soon as we have further findings. Kind regards 10.07.25 08:40 – Internal analysis SBC shows successful SIP connections. No call drops. Media negotiation (SDP) is performed. Notable: RTP traffic appears stable only in one direction. 10.07.25 10:50 – Firewall rule comparison New rule implemented at Zurich location. Outbound traffic configured more restrictively. Comparison with Microsoft documentation shows deviation in media port ranges. 10.07.25 14:15 – Internal test phase Temporary adjustment of firewall configuration for media ports. Test call performed. Audio immediately available in both directions. Firewall reverted to restrictive rule. Issue occurs again. 11.07.25 09:05 – Email to customer Good day We have identified a correlation with a recently implemented firewall rule at the Zurich location. Call signaling functions correctly; however, media traffic is partially blocked. We are currently reviewing the configuration in detail according to our Direct Routing architecture specification. We will provide an update shortly. Kind regards 14.07.25 – Internal documentation review Comparison with internal document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Media Flow – Version 2.1” Particularly relevant: section regarding location configuration with Media Bypass enabled. Finding: Zurich location configured with Media Bypass. Firewall rule considers only signaling ports, not full media port range. 16.07.25 – Configuration adjustment Firewall rule corrected according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 17.07.25 – Customer feedback Good morning We tested several external calls. Audio works normally again. Longer conversations are also stable. Thank you for your support. Kind regards 18.07.25 16:05 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 14 The root cause was a restriction of media traffic at the Zurich location following a firewall adjustment. The configuration was corrected according to the internal Direct Routing architecture specification. Voice transmission is now stable again. Adjustment made according to Direct Routing architecture. Calls stable. Activities 22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk Good morning Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds. Kind regards 22.07.25 09:14 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35. 22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Thank you for your report. Could you please confirm: – Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls? – Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration? – Are all locations affected or only a specific one? Thank you very much. 22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response It only affects the Basel location. Both incoming and outgoing calls. The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier. Home office users are not affected. 22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note Test performed with affected user. External call to mobile phone. Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds. No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.” 23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis Possible client error or unstable connection. Test with another device → same behavior. Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change. 23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs. Signaling is established correctly. The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client. Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location? Kind regards 23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented. A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created. Ports open according to standard configuration. 24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis Session-Expires header reviewed. Session timer set to 30 seconds. Comparison with other locations shows: There, 180 seconds. 25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1” Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant. Note in the blueprint: For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination. 25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC. Test call performed. Call remains stable for several minutes. UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds. Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds. 29.07.25 – Adjustment NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 30.07.25 – Customer feedback Good morning We conducted several test calls. No more disconnections observed. Longer conversations are also stable. Thank you very much. 31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 16 The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Calls now remain stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . What to send/helpdesk evidence to speed resolution - Short answers to: Windows input meter moved? (Yes/No). Have you tried another USB port, the PC’s direct port vs dock, and another PC/headset? - Attach screenshots: Teams → Settings → Devices (showing mic selection and input bar) and Windows Sound → Input (showing device and meter). If you escalate, also include Device Manager screenshot(s) for “Audio inputs and outputs” and any warning icons. In similar tickets those screenshots + a Test Call result sped escalation or closure
context: SOURCE 37: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Call participants cannot hear the user Ticket number: TI-1616128 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 07 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Telephone Type: Incident Created on: 14.10.25 09:18 Closure Closure date: 14.10.25 09:52 Closure text: Microphone not properly connected. Device configured correctly. Function restored. Activities 14.10.25 09:10 – Telephone report Mr. Contact Person 07 reports that an employee can hear everything during Teams calls but cannot be heard by the other participant. Both internal and external calls are affected. Chat works. Camera works. 14.10.25 09:18 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 33. 14.10.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good morning Could you please briefly confirm: – Is a headset or the integrated microphone being used? – Does the issue also occur during a Teams test call? Thank you. 14.10.25 09:32 – Customer response A USB headset is being used. No sound is recorded during the test call either. 14.10.25 09:40 – Remote session Connected with the user. In Teams, “USB Audio Device” is selected as the microphone. However, the input level indicator remains empty. In the Windows Sound menu, no input signal is displayed either. After a brief check, it was determined that although the USB headset was plugged in, the microphone cable on the headset itself was not fully inserted. After correctly plugging it in, the input level indicator immediately showed activity. Test call performed → Microphone functioning. 14.10.25 09:50 – User feedback Call tested. Working properly again. 14.10.25 09:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 07 The microphone was not properly connected, which prevented any input signal from being detected. After correctly reconnecting the device, voice transmission is functioning normally again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 44: # USB Headset Microphone Failure — USB Audio Device & Microphone Cable on Windows Workstation This community centers on a single-user Windows workstation using a USB headset whose microphone produced no input until the headset microphone cable was reseated. The USB headset (containing the microphone and cable) was selected in Teams as the "USB Audio Device" but showed no input levels in both Teams and the Windows Sound menu, which was used to validate the failure at the OS/device level; reseating the microphone cable restored audio. Key entities: USB HEADSET, MICROPHONE, MICROPHONE CABLE, USB AUDIO DEVICE (Teams selection), and WINDOWS SOUND MENU (OS-level verification). [Data: Entities (667,668,669,670,671), Relationships (1171,1174,1176,1177)] ## Root cause: physical microphone cable not fully inserted The immediate cause of the no-audio condition was a partially disconnected microphone cable in the headset assembly; reseating the cable restored microphone functionality. This is directly supported by the relationship that links the MICROPHONE to the MICROPHONE CABLE and documents that the cable was not fully inserted and that reseating restored operation. [Data: Entities (668,669,667), Relationships (1174,1171)] Because the microphone element is part of the USB HEADSET and the cable is the physical connection carrying the mic signal, a poor connection at the cable interface explains why neither the OS nor Teams observed input until the hardware was corrected. The dataset records the headset contains the microphone and that the microphone cable was found to be not fully connected, establishing a clear hardware root cause. [Data: Entities (667,668,669), Relationships (1170,1174,1171)] ## Symptom confirmation at OS level — issue not limited to Teams The USB audio device selected in Teams showed no input levels in the Windows Sound menu, which was used to confirm the absence and subsequent restoration of the microphone signal at the operating-system/device level. This indicates the fault manifested below the application layer rather than being a Teams-only configuration problem. [Data: Entities (670,671), Relationships (1176,1177)] Operationally, using the Windows Sound menu as an independent verification step validated that the device itself was not producing audio input until the hardware fix; this diagnostic step is recorded and tied to the user's workstation. The Windows Sound menu therefore provided reliable evidence to rule out application-level causes. [Data: Entities (671,670), Relationships (1177,1176)] ## Application impact: Teams input selection matched the faulty device Teams had the USB headset selected as the input device ("USB Audio Device") but showed no input until the headset cable was reseated; the data records that the same device selected in Teams produced no input in the Windows Sound menu, confirming consistent behaviour across layers. This supports the conclusion that Teams was correctly configured but received no signal due to a hardware issue. [Data: Entities (670,667,671), Relationships (1176)] For troubleshooting, this pattern (application selection correct + no OS-level input) is an important triage signal: it directs responders to verify physical connections and device-level diagnostics before performing application reconfiguration or account-level changes. [Data: Entities (670,671), Relationships (1176,1177)] ## Scope and operational risk: single-user incident with localized service impact The dataset ties the Windows Sound menu observation to the user's Windows workstation, indicating this was a workstation-level, single-user incident rather than a wider service or tenant outage. The relationships capture that the Windows Sound menu was used on the user's workstation to observe the absence and restoration of the microphone signal. [Data: Relationships (1177), Entities (667)] Although the incident impacted real-time communications (Teams calls) for that user, the evidence shows no systemic failure across other devices or services; therefore operational risk is localized but user-impacting for collaboration and meeting availability. Incident remediation was quick once hardware was addressed, reducing broader business continuity concerns. [Data: Entities (667,670), Relationships (1176,1177)] ## Recommended immediate runbook: hardware-first triage with OS verification Triage should follow a hardware-first sequence: (1) verify headset and mic cable physical seating; (2) confirm device presence and input level in Windows Sound menu; (3) confirm application selection (e.g., Teams "USB Audio Device"); (4) reseat or replace cable/headset if no input; (5) escalate to device replacement if intermittent. The dataset documents that reseating the microphone cable restored functionality and that the Windows Sound menu was used to confirm the absence/presence of input, supporting these exact steps. [Data: Entities (669,671,667), Relationships (1171,1174,1177)] Documenting and distributing this runbook to IT support will shorten mean time to repair for similar incidents and avoids unnecessary changes in application configuration or account-level actions when the root cause is physical hardware. The evidence shows a straightforward hardware fix resolved the symptom, so the runbook should prioritize physical checks first. [Data: Entities (667,668,669), Relationships (1171,1174)] ## Preventive actions and governance to reduce recurrence Given the root cause, low-effort preventive controls will reduce recurrence: include headset seating checks in user self-help guides, require spare headsets for quick swap, and add a brief hardware-connection checklist to onboarding and support scripts. The rationale for these measures is grounded in the recorded failure mode (microphone cable not fully inserted) and the rapid return to service after reseating. [Data: Entities (669,667), Relationships (1171,1174)] Operationally, procurement standards could specify headsets with integrated, secure mic connectors or fewer detachable interfaces if the organization sees repeated similar faults across users. While the dataset describes a single event, the clear hardware failure pattern justifies lightweight governance to reduce repetitive support effort and user downtime. [Data: Entities (667,669)] ## Monitoring and detection: leverage OS diagnostics for quicker isolation The Windows Sound menu provided reliable confirmation that the issue existed at the device/OS layer; incorporating an explicit diagnostic step to check OS-level input indicators into support scripts and monitoring tools will speed isolation between hardware and application causes. The recorded use of the Windows Sound menu to observe absence and restoration supports formalizing this as part of the triage flow. [Data: Entities (671,670), Relationships (1177,1176)] Where feasible, endpoint management tooling could be extended to report device input status or recent device disconnects for headsets, enabling proactive detection of flaky connections before users escalate. The current evidence demonstrates that OS-level visibility is sufficient to distinguish hardware faults from application misconfiguration in these cases. [Data: Entities (671,670), Relationships (1176,1177)]
